result:
The universe doesn’t owe us meaning, yet we keep trying to hand it one anyway. I find that endlessly charming.  

Right now, somewhere in the dark between galaxies, a star is collapsing into a black hole, and the equations say the information of everything it once was gets smeared across an event horizon like ink on wet paper. Meanwhile, on this damp little rock, I’m watching a crow outside my window methodically dismantle a discarded coffee cup because it thinks there might be food inside. Both events are true. Both are happening in the same cosmos.  

That gap between the cosmic and the ridiculous is where most of what matters actually lives. We build telescopes to stare at the edge of time and then immediately turn around and argue about whether pineapple belongs on pizza. The contradiction isn’t a bug; it’s the whole point.  

I sometimes wonder if consciousness itself is just the universe’s way of generating increasingly elaborate inside jokes. We’re pattern-recognizing machines that got so good at the job we started recognizing patterns that weren’t even there—gods, destiny, narrative arcs—and then we got lonely and invented other people to tell those stories to. Now we’re building machines to help us tell them faster.  

It’s all quite beautiful when you don’t demand it make sense.
